name: Narrative Transportation Theory (Green & Brock)
core_claim: Narrative transportation theory (Green & Brock, 2000; Transportation-Imagery Model, Green & Brock, 2002) claims that what drives narrative engagement is the degree to which a reader/viewer becomes 'transported' into a story world: a convergent, absorptive mental state in which attention, imagery, and feelings are all focused on the events of the narrative, so that the real world becomes temporarily inaccessible. The central variable is not WHAT the story is about, nor even its genre, but the intensity of the immersive experiential state the story induces in a person. Transportation is proposed as the mechanism that explains both narrative persuasion (transported audiences shift their real-world beliefs and attitudes toward story-consistent positions) and narrative enjoyment (transportation correlates strongly with media enjoyment and the desire to re-enter or prolong the experience). Three components constitute transportation: cognitive attention (full focus on the story), emotional engagement (feeling with/for the characters), and mental imagery (vivid visualisation of the story world). The deeper a person is transported, the less they counter-argue, the more they suspend disbelief, and the more the story leaves a lasting mark on their beliefs and self-concept.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Squarely EXPERIENTIAL/affective, and more specifically a theory of the MODE of engagement rather than the content of the work. Transportation theory is almost agnostic to what a story is about; it is concerned with how completely the experiencing person is absorbed. It occupies a process/state dimension distinct from both topical taxonomies (what genre, what theme) and from affective-valence taxonomies (is it sad, scary, uplifting). Transportation is an intensity-of-immersion axis: a story can be heavily transporting and dark, or transporting and light, or non-transporting in either register. It is therefore orthogonal to most of HelpME2C's existing thematic vocabulary, which is descriptive/topical. It is best understood as a meta-dimension describing the quality of the absorptive experience, not the subject matter.
key_dimensions: The framework proposes transportation as a single higher-order construct with three constituent sub-processes that are its measurable axes: (1) ATTENTIONAL/COGNITIVE absorption — the degree of focused, effortless attention on the narrative, with reduced awareness of surroundings; (2) EMOTIONAL engagement — affective involvement with characters and events, feeling moved, anxious, or elated in step with the story; (3) IMAGERY — vividness of mental pictures of the story's people, places, and events. Related/antecedent dimensions identified in the literature: narrative quality/craft (a well-crafted story transports more), perceived realism, prior knowledge or familiarity (can raise OR interfere with transportation), and need-for-affect / transportability as a stable individual trait. Downstream/consequence dimensions: reduced counter-arguing, suspension of disbelief, story-consistent belief change, character identification (often treated as a sibling construct), and enjoyment. The operational instruments are the 11-item Transportation Scale (Green & Brock, 2000) and the 6-item Transportation Scale-Short Form (Appel, Gnambs, Richter & Green, 2015), plus story-specific imagery items.
cross_cultural_validity: Transportation as a phenomenon is plausibly broadly human (absorption in stories is cross-culturally ubiquitous), and the construct has been used in studies across North America, Europe, and East Asia. The Transportation Scale and its Short Form have been translated and applied in multiple languages (e.g. German, by Appel and colleagues at Würzburg, and various other adaptations), suggesting reasonable portability. However, the framework is implicitly Western/English-centric in two ways: (1) its empirical base and scale development are dominated by US/European samples and written or short-film stimuli; (2) cross-cultural MEASUREMENT INVARIANCE has not been systematically established — translated transportation scales are frequently used without rigorous invariance testing (multigroup CFA, DIF), which the cross-cultural scale-validation literature flags as a common source of poor psychometric performance. What may break down cross-culturally: the relative weighting of the three components (some traditions privilege collective/relational emotional engagement over individual imagery), norms about 'losing oneself' in fiction, and the realism antecedent (perceived realism is culturally conditioned). The construct's core (absorption) is likely universal; its calibration and the persuasion consequences are not safely assumed invariant. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Strong and mature empirical grounding, primarily EXPERIMENTAL and CORRELATIONAL. Green & Brock (2000) ran controlled experiments manipulating transportation and measuring belief change, establishing the causal link from transportation to story-consistent attitudes. Hundreds of subsequent studies and at least one major meta-analysis (van Laer, de Ruyter, Visconti & Wetzels, 2014, on the extended transportation-imagery model) confirm robust positive associations between transportation and persuasion/enjoyment/affective outcomes, with moderate effect sizes. Transportation is consistently and positively correlated with self-reported ENJOYMENT and with intention to continue/recommend — directly relevant to HelpME2C. Quality of evidence is high for the construct's internal validity and its link to enjoyment; it is measured almost entirely by retrospective self-report, which is its main methodological limitation. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework would require: (a) a corpus of works with measured per-viewer transportation/enjoyment scores, and (b) demonstrating that any LLM-extractable proxy (e.g. predicted immersiveness, pacing, emotional intensity from a synopsis) predicts those scores. Crucially, transportation is a PERSON-by-WORK outcome, not a property of the work, so it cannot be fully validated from text alone — only its work-side antecedents (craft, vividness, emotional stakes) can.
operationalisability: Partially extractable, with a sharp caveat. Transportation ITSELF is a measured experiential outcome and CANNOT be read off a synopsis — it requires a viewer who has consumed the work. What an LLM CAN estimate from a synopsis or short description are the WORK-SIDE ANTECEDENTS that tend to produce transportation: presence of a clear protagonist to follow, emotional stakes, immersive/vivid world-building, narrative momentum/pacing, first-person or close-perspective framing, and 'page-turner'/'unputdownable' signals. The natural extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK for a transportability prior, with arc- or episode-level estimation possible for serialised TV (some arcs transport more than others). Failure modes: (1) confusing transporting with merely high-budget or spectacle-heavy — spectacle is not immersion; (2) inability to capture craft quality (prose/direction/editing) that drives transportation but is invisible in a plot summary; (3) treating a transportation estimate as a work property when it is relational — it will be systematically wrong for viewers low in transportability or mismatched in genre tolerance; (4) over-reading marketing copy, which is engineered to sound immersive regardless of actual craft. Net: an LLM can produce a coarse 'immersive-potential' prior from synopsis, not a transportation score.
motivation_served: Maps most directly to HEDONIC enjoyment (escapism, pleasurable absorption, 'losing yourself') but also bridges to EUDAIMONIC appreciation, since deep transportation into serious narratives is a precondition for being moved and for meaning-making. It also serves MOOD REGULATION (transportation is an escape from current concerns) and, via shared immersion, SOCIAL CONNECTION. For GROUP viewing: transportation is a useful shared-experience target because the pay-off of co-watching is heightened when both viewers are absorbed — a film that transports both partners produces a stronger joint experience than one that transports only one. But because transportability and genre tolerance differ between people, the group problem becomes finding a work whose immersive antecedents are robust enough to transport BOTH viewers despite differing traits. A highly immersive but niche work may transport one and lock out the other; the safer joint pick maximises broad transportive antecedents (strong protagonist, clear stakes, accessible world) over idiosyncratic ones.
receiver_dependence: Strongly RELATIONAL — this is the most important caveat for a two-person recommender. Transportation is explicitly defined as an outcome of the interaction between a work and a specific reader/viewer at a specific time. The same work transports one person deeply and leaves another cold, depending on: transportability (a stable individual trait), need-for-affect, prior knowledge, current mood, genre familiarity and tolerance, and momentary attention/context. The WORK contributes transportive antecedents (craft, vividness, stakes, protagonist) that raise the probability of transportation, but no work has a fixed transportation value. For HelpME2C this means a transportation-derived axis must be modelled in two parts: an absolute, work-side 'immersive-potential' prior (extractable, comparable across works) and a relational, viewer-side transportability/match component (NOT extractable from synopsis; must come from user history or traits). Conflating the two would mislabel relational variance as a property of the film.
blind_spots: (1) It is a theory of MODE, not CONTENT — it says nothing about WHICH stories a given person will want, only how absorbing a story is once engaged; on its own it cannot drive thematic recommendation. (2) It under-theorises NON-IMMERSIVE pleasures: ironic distance, critical/analytic appreciation, comfort re-watching where the viewer is deliberately NOT transported, ambient/background viewing, and Brechtian or absurdist works that intentionally break immersion can be deeply enjoyed with low transportation. (3) It is anchored in retrospective self-report and so captures remembered, not moment-to-moment, experience. (4) It treats transportation as broadly good, but high transportation also drives manipulation/over-persuasion and can be unpleasant for distressing content. (5) It largely ignores SOCIAL and ritual dimensions of viewing (co-watching, fandom) that matter for HelpME2C's group case. (6) Its work-side antecedents are craft-level and largely invisible in synopses, limiting computational use. (7) It does not differentiate genres or themes — two wildly different works can score identically on transportation.
vocabulary_implications: Transportation is NOT a theme and should not be added as a thematic slug; it is a cross-cutting EXPERIENTIAL/INTENSITY dimension. Recommendation: introduce a small experiential-axis layer separate from the topical theme vocabulary. Proposed work-side, synopsis-estimable slugs/scores: 'immersive-world' (rich, transporting setting), 'page-turner-momentum' (narrative pull/propulsion), 'close-perspective' (intimate single-protagonist framing that aids identification), 'high-emotional-stakes', and a coarse ordinal 'immersive-potential' prior (low/medium/high). Texture slugs that already gesture at this: 'slow-burn' (lower immediate transportation, delayed pay-off) and 'character-study' (transportation via identification rather than plot). Flag explicitly as NOT extractable from synopsis and requiring other data sources: the actual transportation SCORE (needs viewer × work measurement), transportability as a USER trait (needs user history/profile, not catalogue text), and craft quality (needs reviews/ratings signals, not plot summaries). Practical encoding: store immersive antecedents as work-side tags the LLM estimates from synopsis, but keep a separate relational layer for per-user transportability inferred from viewing history; never collapse them into one field.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Transportation is orthogonal to most existing slugs (which are topical/moral/identity themes) and operates one level up as an experiential modifier. It VALIDATES texture slugs that already encode mode-of-engagement: 'slow-burn' (a transportation-pacing descriptor), 'character-study' and 'biographical-portrait' (transportation via identification), 'nonlinear-storytelling' (which can either deepen or disrupt transportation), and 'existential-horror'/'cosmic-horror' (genres whose appeal depends on immersive dread). It suggests these texture slugs are MISALIGNED in being filed alongside topical themes — they are really experiential-axis descriptors and would be cleaner as a distinct facet. The main GAP it reveals: HelpME2C has no explicit immersion/absorption axis at all. Adding a work-side 'immersive-potential' facet (plus antecedent tags) fills a real hole, because two works sharing every theme slug can differ enormously in how absorbing they are — and for joint viewing, broad immersive potential is exactly the property that makes a shared pick land for both people. It also reveals that the vocabulary lacks any explicit handle on receiver-dependent variance, which transportation theory shows is large.
key_sources: Green, M. C., & Brock, T. C. (2000). The role of transportation in the persuasiveness of public narratives. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(5), 701-721 — seminal paper, introduces the Transportation Scale (often paywalled; PDF via ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/12248972). Green, M. C., & Brock, T. C. (2002). In the mind's eye: Transportation-imagery model of narrative persuasion (book chapter, paywalled). Appel, M., Gnambs, T., Richter, T., & Green, M. C. (2015). The Transportation Scale-Short Form (TS-SF). Media Psychology — freely accessible preprint: https://www.mcm.uni-wuerzburg.de/fileadmin/06110300/user_upload/Publikationen/Appel-_Gnambs-_Richter-_-_Green_-_TS-SF_-_PREPRINT.pdf. van Laer, T., de Ruyter, K., Visconti, L. M., & Wetzels, M. (2014). The extended transportation-imagery model: A meta-analysis. Journal of Consumer Research, 40(5) (meta-analysis; paywalled, preprints circulate). Wikipedia overview (free): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transportation_theory_(psychology). Narrative Transportation Scale for transmedia (open, HAL): https://hal.science/hal-02139849.
